content:
4 min read
1 hour ago

--

So yeah, I shipped a lot this week. Four projects, one stretch of days, and more bugs than I’d like to admit finding after I thought things were done. The stuff that’s silently broken never tells you; you have to go build the thing that tells you.
Deadhold: Ruins, Tutorials, and Actually Testing Your Art Pipeline
The ruin system is finally done end-to-end. And I mean actually done, hashed state, a real clear-and-reindex flow, and tests that got re-baselined on purpose instead of by accident. That last part matters more than it sounds. Accidental baselines are just future surprises with extra steps.
Then the tutorial. Built it from nothing: the step-by-step engine, in-world guidance overlays, the “build this first” content structure. All of it. But building it wasn’t the part that took time. The part that took time was two full passes hunting down every softlock before a real player ever touches it. Softlocks are the kind of failure that doesn’t announce itself either. A player just… stops. Gets stuck. Quits. And you never find out why unless you went looking first.
Two passes and I found stuff in both. That’s the whole point.
And then there was the art pipeline question I had been putting off for a while. Three different approaches were on the table, and I could have just picked the one that sounded best on paper. I didn’t do that; I ran a real bake-off, actual test sets, actual results, and picked the one that survived. Not the one that felt right theoretically. The one that worked when I made it prove itself.
That’s a different decision than most people make, and I think it’s the right one.
Timbre: The Voice Loop That Wasn’t Working in Production
Timbre’s voice-learning loop is real now. Every edit you make to a post actually trains your profile; that’s the pitch, and it’s not a pitch anymore; it’s what the code does. But here’s the part that would have been embarrassing: it wasn’t working in production.
Four separate silent failures. Four. None of them announced themselves. The feature looked fine from the outside. If I hadn’t run a deliberate post-deploy check, specifically looking for things that could be broken, none of it would have surfaced until a user ran into it and had no idea why their profile wasn’t learning anything.
So yeah, four days of that. Finding things. Fixing things. Making sure the thing I shipped was actually the thing I thought I shipped.
The gap between “I implemented this” and “this works in production” is where silent bugs live. And they are not going to come out on their own.## On the Tip: Code-Complete, 177 Puzzles, Waiting on Apple
On Tip is done. Code-complete, final hardening pass shipped, guess-checker got stricter, 177 puzzles seeded and ready. The game is there.
What’s left isn’t code; it’s paperwork. My Apple developer account needs to convert from individual to company status before I can submit under my LLC. That’s the blocker right now. Not a bug, not a feature gap. Just an account conversion sitting between the finished product and the App Store.
Anywho. It’ll get there.
ELI5: When Nobody Owns the Visual Style
ELI5 had a problem I had been kind of avoiding. Every episode looked like a different show. The hand-drawn UI and the AI-illustrated art were getting composited together with no single person, or rule, or reference controlling how a frame should actually look. Nobody owned the visual style. So every episode made its own decisions, and those decisions didn’t agree with each other.
Tested a fix: lock one reference image as the style anchor for an entire episode. Everything gets checked against that image. The first clip made with that approach beat all six earlier versions. Not close.
So the issue wasn’t the art itself. It was the absence of a decision about who was in charge of consistency. One reference image per episode. That’s the whole fix and it took testing to actually confirm it worked, not just assuming it would.
That’s a Wrap
Same lesson across all four of these. The thing that’s silently broken is never going to tell you it’s broken. Deadhold’s softlocks weren’t going to surface themselves. Timbre’s four production failures weren’t going to announce themselves. ELI5’s visual drift wasn’t going to fix itself by someone eventually noticing it felt off.
You have to go find it. Build the check. Run the bake-off. Do the second pass. Make the thing that tells you what’s wrong before your users do.
Hope this helps, or at least makes you go poke at something in your own project that you’ve been assuming is fine.
Later!
